blocks.html:
When a tool fails, the error message isn't just diagnostics — it's instructions to the agent about what to do next. A vague "Error: try again" forces the model to guess: retry? ask the user? give up? Production tools classify their failures so the agent's next move is obvious.

Five categories cover nearly everything: transient infrastructure (timeout, 503 — retry inside the tool), permanent validation (bad date, malformed ID — tell the agent exactly what's wrong so it can fix the input), business rule (not eligible — non-retryable, explain to the user), permission (no access — non-retryable, offer the escalation path), and the dangerous one: uncertain write state.

The rule of thumb for retries: put retry logic where the information to fix the problem lives. The tool knows a timeout is transient — it retries. Only the model can fix a bad filter string — surface it. Only a human can authorize risking a duplicate charge — escalate it.

Your send_invoice_email tool calls a mail API that sometimes times out after the send was submitted. If the tool says "Error: timeout, please retry," the agent retries — and the customer gets two invoices. The right return communicates uncertainty: "Delivery status unknown. The message may have been sent. Do not retry without an idempotency check — verify via status lookup or ask the user." Read-side timeouts and write-after-submission timeouts are inverses: the first is safe to retry, the second may duplicate a real-world effect.

MCP adds a two-tier error model that exams love:

  • Protocol errors (JSON-RPC): the call itself was invalid — unknown tool, missing schema-required parameter. The failure happened before business logic ran.
  • Tool execution errors (isError: true): the tool ran and the operation failed — upstream 404, 503, permission denial, business rule.

Misusing the tiers causes specific misbehavior: missing-parameter failures shoved into isError make agents retry identical bad calls; a 503 raised as a protocol error stops the agent from retrying something that would have succeeded later. Also memorize the pitfalls: never return an empty list for a backend failure (it reads as "success, nothing found"), and never throw exceptions for expected business errors (frameworks hide the details from the model).

items: Classify failures — the category tells the agent its next move.
Retry lives where the fix lives: tool (transient), model (bad inputs), human (risky side effects).
Write-after-submission timeout = uncertain state: report uncertainty, block auto-retry, verify.
MCP: protocol error before business logic; isError: true when the operation failed.
Empty results mean 'nothing found,' never 'the API failed.'
